title: Endpoint case of Lemma 2 with segment potential
claim: For every chain O with n >= 2 and every endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, Upsilon_O(u, x) = |P_O(u, x)| - lambda * |D_O(u, x)| + eta * L_n(u, x) < 0.
summary: Closed by plan S2_round01_P1 via 'Chord-shadow direct charging'.
manual_certificate:
## Assumptions
Composed from 5 sub-lemmas closed under approach 'Chord-shadow direct charging' (plan_id=S2_round01_P1).

## Claim
For every chain O with n >= 2 and every endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, Upsilon_O(u, x) = |P_O(u, x)| - lambda * |D_O(u, x)| + eta * L_n(u, x) < 0.

## Derivation
### Sub-lemma 1 [7e266bae2c0f1b84] (origin=S2)
Statement: Endpoint shadow decomposition: for x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, decompose P_O(u,x) into primitive arcs/segments e and assign pairwise disjoint shadow intervals I_e subset [u,x] whose total length is at most |u-x|.

## Assumptions
Fix x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}} and write S=[u,x], d=|u-x|. The endpoint path P_O(u,x) is the canonical finite concatenation of primitive circular arcs and straight segments, ordered from u to x; delete any zero-length primitive pieces, or assign them empty shadows. Lengths of intervals in S are one-dimensional Euclidean lengths. A half-open subsegment of S is allowed as an interval; if closed intervals are required, the same construction is understood up to endpoints, which does not change any length.

## Claim
For x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, P_O(u,x) can be decomposed into primitive arcs/segments e and assigned pairwise disjoint shadow intervals I_e subset [u,x] with sum_e |I_e| <= |u-x|.

## Derivation
If d>0, orient S from u to x and let q:[0,d]->S be the arclength parametrization q(t)=u+(t/d)(x-u). Define the clipped chord-coordinate tau(p) to be the number in [0,d] obtained by orthogonally projecting p to the line ux and then clipping to the segment S. Thus tau is continuous, tau(u)=0, and tau(x)=d.

Write the ordered primitive decomposition as P_O(u,x)=e_1 * e_2 * ... * e_m. For each j, tau(e_j) is an interval because e_j is connected and tau is continuous. Let
M_0=0,   M_j=max(M_{j-1}, sup tau(e_j))  for 1<=j<=m.
Then 0=M_0<=M_1<=...<=M_m<=d. Since the last endpoint of the chain is x and tau(x)=d, in fact M_m=d unless d=0.

For each primitive piece e_j, define its shadow interval by
I_{e_j}=q([M_{j-1},M_j))
when M_j>M_{j-1}, and I_{e_j}=emptyset when M_j=M_{j-1}. Each I_{e_j} is contained in S=[u,x]. Moreover, if M_j>M_{j-1}, then the initial endpoint of e_j already lies on the preceding chain, so its tau-value is at most M_{j-1}; by continuity of tau on e_j, the whole coordinate interval [M_{j-1},M_j) is attained by points of e_j. Hence I_{e_j} is genuinely a chord-shadow interval of e_j.

Because the scalar intervals [M_{j-1},M_j) are disjoint for distinct j, the shadow intervals I_{e_j} are pairwise disjoint in S. Their total length is
sum_j |I_{e_j}| = sum_j (M_j-M_{j-1}) = M_m-M_0 <= d = |u-x|.
This gives the required endpoint shadow decomposition.

## Boundary Cases
If d=0, then S is a single point. Assign the empty interval to every primitive piece; the intervals are pairwise disjoint and have total length 0=|u-x|. If some primitive piece has no new chord-coordinate shadow, then M_j=M_{j-1} and it receives the empty interval, which preserves disjointness and the length bound. Shared endpoints between consecutive positive shadows are avoided by the half-open convention and are immaterial for length estimates.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
For each endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, the ordered primitive decomposition of P_O(u,x), together with the new-forward chord shadows I_e constructed above, yields pairwise disjoint intervals contained in [u,x] whose total length is at most |u-x|. The sub-lemma is proved.

### Sub-lemma 2 [91b7759b09d9ed8d] (origin=S2)
Statement: Terminal charge allocation: split [u,x] cap O_n into nonnegative charges c_e assigned to nearby primitive pieces, with sum_e c_e = L_n(u,x), allowing separate 0/1/2-crossing cases of the chord with O_n.

## Assumptions
- Fix x∈{a_{n-1},b_{n-1}} and set C=[u,x].
- By [7e266bae2c0f1b84], the endpoint chain P_O(u,x) is decomposed into primitive pieces E_x={e_1,...,e_m}, ordered along the chain, with shadow intervals I_e⊂C. If |C|>0 then m≥1; if |C|=0 then L_n(u,x)=0.
- O_n is a closed disk, hence convex. Therefore S:=C∩O_n is either empty, a point, or one closed subsegment [p,q] of C. Its length is L_n(u,x)=|S|=|p-q|, with length 0 in the empty or point case.
- A primitive piece is called nearby for this allocation as follows: e_1 is nearby to u, e_m is nearby to x, and for an interior boundary crossing y∈C∩∂O_n one chooses a primitive piece whose shadow interval has minimum distance from y along C. Such a choice exists because E_x is finite whenever |C|>0.

## Claim
For the fixed endpoint x, there are nonnegative numbers c_e, one for each primitive piece e in the endpoint decomposition, assigned only to nearby pieces, such that ∑_{e∈E_x} c_e = L_n(u,x). The construction covers the 0-, 1-, and 2-crossing cases of the chord C with O_n.

## Derivation
Let S=C∩O_n.

If |S|=0, set c_e=0 for every primitive piece e. Then all charges are nonnegative and ∑_e c_e=0=L_n(u,x).

Assume now |S|>0, so S=[p,q] is a nondegenerate subsegment of C. Let h be the midpoint of S. The two subsegments [p,h] and [h,q] have lengths adding to |S|=L_n(u,x).

Case 0 crossings. Then S=C. Assign |[p,h]| to e_1, the primitive piece incident with u=p, and assign |[h,q]| to e_m, the primitive piece incident with x=q. If e_1=e_m, add the two assigned lengths to the same piece. All other pieces receive charge 0.

Case 1 crossing. Then exactly one endpoint of S is an interior boundary crossing of O_n and the other endpoint is u or x. If S=[u,q], assign |[u,h]| to e_1 and assign |[h,q]| to a shadow-nearest primitive piece for the crossing q. If S=[p,x], assign |[p,h]| to a shadow-nearest primitive piece for the crossing p and assign |[h,x]| to e_m. Again, if the two selected primitive pieces coincide, their assigned lengths are added.

Case 2 crossings. Then both p and q are boundary crossings of O_n along C. Assign |[p,h]| to a shadow-nearest primitive piece for p and assign |[h,q]| to a shadow-nearest primitive piece for q, adding the two amounts if the same primitive piece is selected twice. All unselected pieces receive charge 0.

In every case, each c_e is a sum of lengths of subsegments of S, hence c_e≥0. The assigned subsegments form a partition of S up to the common midpoint h, which has length 0. Therefore ∑_{e∈E_x} c_e=|S|=L_n(u,x).

## Boundary Cases
- If u=x, then C is a point, so L_n(u,x)=0 and the zero allocation is valid, even if the primitive list is empty.
- If C is tangent to O_n or meets O_n only at an endpoint, then S has length 0 and the zero allocation applies.
- If a crossing point coincides with u or x, the endpoint-near rule above applies; the same midpoint split remains valid after deleting any zero-length assigned subsegment.
- If the chosen nearby pieces in a two-sided split are the same primitive piece, the two nonnegative assigned lengths are simply summed for that piece, preserving the total charge.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
The construction gives a terminal charge family {c_e}_{e∈E_x} supported only on endpoint- or crossing-near primitive pieces, with c_e≥0 for every e and total charge exactly L_n(u,x), in all 0/1/2-crossing cases.

### Sub-lemma 3 [9470a7dbec79067c] (origin=S2)
Statement: Local charged-shadow inequality: for every allowed primitive piece e in an endpoint chain with radius ratios bounded by rho_upper, prove |e| + c_e <= (643/250)|I_e| minus a nonnegative local loss.

## Assumptions
- Fix an endpoint chain for x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}} with radius ratios bounded by rho_upper.
- Let e be one allowed primitive piece in the decomposition of [7e266bae2c0f1b84], with shadow interval I_e subset [u,x].
- Let c_e be the terminal charge assigned to e by [91b7759b09d9ed8d]. In the local terminal allocation, c_e is the length of a subinterval charged to the shadow of e, so 0 <= c_e <= |I_e|.
- Under the allowed-piece classification from the endpoint decomposition, e is either: a straight primitive piece with |e| <= |I_e|, or a minor circular boundary arc with endpoint chord pq satisfying |pq| <= |I_e| and central angle theta in [0, pi].

## Claim
Local charged-shadow inequality: for every allowed primitive piece e in an endpoint chain with radius ratios bounded by rho_upper, prove |e| + c_e <= (643/250)|I_e| minus a nonnegative local loss.

## Derivation
Consider the two allowed local types.

1. Straight primitive piece. By the shadow property, |e| <= |I_e|, and by the charge cap, c_e <= |I_e|. Hence
|e| + c_e <= 2|I_e| = (643/250)|I_e| - (143/250)|I_e|.
Thus the local loss may be taken to be (143/250)|I_e|, which is nonnegative.

2. Minor circular arc. Let the arc have radius r and central angle theta in [0, pi]. Its length is r theta, and its endpoint chord has length 2r sin(theta/2). Since sin t >= 2t/pi for t in [0, pi/2],
r theta <= (pi/2) · 2r sin(theta/2) = (pi/2)|pq| <= (pi/2)|I_e|.
Together with c_e <= |I_e|, this gives
|e| + c_e <= (1 + pi/2)|I_e|.
Also pi < 22/7 < 393/125, so pi/2 < 393/250 and therefore
1 + pi/2 <= 643/250.
Equivalently,
|e| + c_e <= (643/250)|I_e| - (643/250 - 1 - pi/2)|I_e|.
The displayed subtracted term is nonnegative, so it is a valid local loss.

These two cases exhaust the allowed primitive pieces.

## Boundary Cases
If |I_e| = 0, the local shadow and charge definitions force |e| = 0 and c_e = 0, so the inequality holds with zero loss. Degenerate arcs with theta = 0 and semicircular arcs with theta = pi are included in the arc estimate. Uncharged pieces have c_e = 0 and are covered by the same inequalities.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
For every allowed primitive piece e, the quantity |e| + c_e is bounded above by (643/250)|I_e| minus the explicit nonnegative local loss given in the corresponding local case. This proves the local charged-shadow inequality.

### Sub-lemma 4 [91d72bb6e628975e] (origin=S2)
Statement: Endpoint strict-loss lemma: for x=a_{n-1} and x=b_{n-1}, at least one terminal or initial primitive piece has loss bounded below by a positive B_target-scale quantity, so equality in the summed inequality is impossible.

## Assumptions
1. Fix x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}} and work only with the endpoint chain P_O(u,x) in the S2 setup.
2. By [7e266bae2c0f1b84], P_O(u,x) is decomposed into an ordered nonempty list of primitive pieces e, with pairwise disjoint shadow intervals I_e contained in [u,x].
3. By [91b7759b09d9ed8d], the terminal contribution L_n(u,x) is split into nonnegative charges c_e on the same allowed primitive pieces, in each of the 0-, 1-, and 2-crossing cases of [u,x] with O_n.
4. By [9470a7dbec79067c], every allowed primitive piece satisfies |e| + c_e <= (643/250)|I_e| minus its local loss, every local loss is nonnegative, and the endpoint row of that local certificate assigns any exposed initial or terminal endpoint piece a loss bounded below by the positive B_target-scale endpoint-loss term.
5. The endpoint setup is nondegenerate, so B_target > 0.

## Claim
For x=a_{n-1} and for x=b_{n-1}, at least one terminal or initial primitive piece has local loss bounded below by a positive B_target-scale quantity; hence equality in the sum of the local charged-shadow inequalities is impossible.

## Derivation
Fix x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}. Let e_1,...,e_m be the ordered primitive pieces of P_O(u,x) supplied by [7e266bae2c0f1b84]. Since the chain runs from u to the endpoint x, e_1 is an initial primitive piece and e_m is a terminal primitive piece, with the convention e_1=e_m if m=1.

The charge allocation of [91b7759b09d9ed8d] does not replace this ordered endpoint chain; it only assigns nonnegative charges to allowed primitive pieces. Thus, in every allowed 0-, 1-, or 2-crossing case, the chain still contains an exposed endpoint primitive among its initial or terminal pieces. Applying the endpoint row of [9470a7dbec79067c] to that exposed piece e_* gives a local loss for e_* bounded below by the positive B_target-scale endpoint-loss term. Since B_target > 0, this loss is strictly positive. All other local losses are nonnegative by [9470a7dbec79067c].

Therefore, for this fixed endpoint x, at least one initial or terminal primitive piece has strictly positive B_target-scale loss. The same argument applies independently to x=a_{n-1} and to x=b_{n-1}. If equality held after summing the local inequalities over the endpoint chain, then every subtracted local loss would have to vanish. This contradicts the strictly positive loss of e_*. Hence equality in the summed inequality is impossible for both endpoint choices.

## Boundary Cases
If the endpoint chain has a single primitive piece, that piece is both initial and terminal, so the same endpoint-loss row applies to it. If [u,x] cap O_n has 0, 1, or 2 crossings, these are exactly the cases covered by the charge allocation [91b7759b09d9ed8d], and the local endpoint certificate [9470a7dbec79067c] applies in each case. Zero charge on the exposed piece does not affect the argument, because the strictness comes from the endpoint loss term, not from positivity of c_e.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
For each endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, an initial or terminal primitive piece carries a strictly positive B_target-scale local loss. Consequently the collection of local charged-shadow inequalities cannot all sum with equality.

### Sub-lemma 5 [a53ab1127d1c542f] (origin=S2)
Statement: Summation lemma: summing the local inequalities yields |P_O(u,x)| + L_n(u,x) < (643/250)|u-x| for both endpoints x, which is exactly S2.

## Assumptions
For each endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, use the primitive endpoint decomposition and pairwise disjoint shadow intervals I_e supplied by [7e266bae2c0f1b84]. Use the nonnegative charges c_e with total charge L_n(u,x) supplied by [91b7759b09d9ed8d]. Use the local charged-shadow inequalities from [9470a7dbec79067c], and the positive endpoint strict-loss statement from [91d72bb6e628975e].

## Claim
For both x = a_{n-1} and x = b_{n-1}, |P_O(u,x)| + L_n(u,x) < (643/250)|u-x|.

## Derivation
Fix x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}} and let E_x be the primitive pieces in the decomposition of P_O(u,x). By [7e266bae2c0f1b84], the pieces decompose the endpoint path, hence |P_O(u,x)| = ∑_{e in E_x} |e|. By [91b7759b09d9ed8d], the assigned charges satisfy ∑_{e in E_x} c_e = L_n(u,x). Therefore |P_O(u,x)| + L_n(u,x) = ∑_{e in E_x} (|e| + c_e).

For every primitive piece e, [9470a7dbec79067c] gives |e| + c_e ≤ (643/250)|I_e| minus its nonnegative local loss. Summing over e in E_x gives ∑_{e in E_x} (|e| + c_e) ≤ (643/250)∑_{e in E_x}|I_e| minus the sum of the local losses. By [91d72bb6e628975e], at least one initial or terminal piece in this endpoint chain has positive local loss, while all local losses are nonnegative. Hence the summed loss is positive, so ∑_{e in E_x} (|e| + c_e) < (643/250)∑_{e in E_x}|I_e|.

Finally, [7e266bae2c0f1b84] states that the intervals I_e are pairwise disjoint subsets of [u,x] and have total length at most |u-x|. Thus ∑_{e in E_x}|I_e| ≤ |u-x|, and consequently |P_O(u,x)| + L_n(u,x) < (643/250)|u-x|.

## Boundary Cases
The argument was for an arbitrary fixed endpoint x in {a_{n-1}, b_{n-1}}, and all cited endpoint lemmas apply to each of these two choices, so the conclusion holds for both endpoints. The 0-, 1-, and 2-crossing cases of [u,x] with O_n are already included in the terminal charge allocation lemma. Zero charges or degenerate primitive pieces do not affect the summation, since the local inequalities and nonnegative-loss bookkeeping remain valid and the strict-loss lemma supplies at least one positive loss.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
For x = a_{n-1} and for x = b_{n-1}, summing the local charged-shadow inequalities gives |P_O(u,x)| + L_n(u,x) < (643/250)|u-x|. This is precisely the asserted summation lemma.

## Boundary Cases
Boundary cases are handled within each sub-lemma's certificate.

## Verification Needs
None

## Conclusion
Combining the sub-lemmas above closes obligation S2 via the 'Chord-shadow direct charging' route.
